Title: The live preview canvas
Summary: WebGL2 canvas that draws your shader live, with aspect-ratio chips and pause/scrub time controls. Two passes per frame: bound texture as opaque base, then your shader composited over.
Body:
## The Live preview tab

The shader edit page splits into two tabs; the **Live preview** tab is the bigger surface that hosts the WebGL2 canvas plus its controls. The canvas itself is centered at `max-w-3xl` (768px wide on a wide monitor), with a dynamic aspect ratio you pick from the toolbar. Below the canvas: the **Test image** picker, then the auto-generated **Uniforms** section.

## The toolbar

A single row above the canvas with two control clusters:

- **Aspect ratio chips** — `1:1` / `16:9` / `4:3` / `21:9`. The default is `16:9` (most game viewports). Your pick is persisted globally to `localStorage` — your monitor and your game's viewport ratio stay the same regardless of which shader you're editing, so a per-app preference makes more sense than per-shader.
- **Time controls** — `⏸ Pause` / `▶ Play` button, a live `u_time` readout (1 decimal), a scrub slider (0..60s, 0.05s step), and a `↺ 0` reset button.

## Pause + scrub

Every shader the preview can render is fundamentally time-based — `u_time` is one of the built-ins the translator auto-injects. At 60Hz it's hard to look at a *specific moment* of a time-driven effect (decay, trails, moving patterns). The Pause button freezes `u_time` at its current value; the scrub slider lets you drag to any specific time. Scrubbing while playing **implicitly pauses**.

When you press Play after a pause, the runtime re-anchors its internal `startTime` so playback resumes at the manualTime value — no visual jump on your eye. ↺ resets `u_time` to 0 in either mode (a calm "rewind" gesture when paused, a "restart" gesture when playing).

## What's drawn

Every animation frame the canvas runs two passes:

1. **Pass 1 (base)** — a fixed passthrough shader draws the bound texture (the image you picked, or the default UV-grid pattern). Opaque, no blending. Establishes a visible base.
2. **Pass 2 (your shader)** — the compiled user shader runs over the base with standard alpha blending (`SRC_ALPHA, ONE_MINUS_SRC_ALPHA`). The framebuffer's alpha is preserved at 1.0 (`blendFuncSeparate`) so the canvas always presents as opaque.

This matches what Godot's compositor does conceptually at the sprite scope: the sprite gets drawn, then your `canvas_item` shader's `COLOR` composites over it. A shader that writes `COLOR = texture(TEXTURE, UV) * tint` (alpha 1.0) fully replaces the base. A shader that writes `COLOR = vec4(0, 0, 0, dim)` (low alpha) dims the base — the scanlines case.

Without the base pass, dimming shaders rendered as nothing because the canvas was transparent everywhere the shader's alpha was low. With the base pass, you always see *something* and the shader's effect on it is visible.

## The default UV grid

The canvas's initial texture is generated procedurally — a 64×64 8-step checkerboard tinted by UV (R = u, G = v, B = 0.5). Two things at once:

- Shaders that **sample TEXTURE** see a recognizable checkerboard so you can tell if the sampling is working.
- Shaders that **ignore TEXTURE** and just compute on UV see the UV-tinted backdrop, so e.g. a scanlines or vignette shader has something visible to dim.

No binary asset ships — it's built in JavaScript at runtime.

## Swapping the test image

The **Test image** section under the canvas is the same [AssetPicker](/help/assets-and-image-editor/gallery) used elsewhere in the app. Pick any image in your Godot project; it loads via `/api/asset?path=...` (the same endpoint that drives `AssetThumb` everywhere) and replaces the bound `u_texture`. Clear the path with the **×** to revert to the default UV grid.

The picked image is per-edit-page state, not persisted. Closing the page resets to the default.

## Compile errors

Diagnostics show up in two places, driven by the same lifted state in the edit page:

- **Red banner under the canvas** with full message text, line-anchored:
  - `line 12: 'foo' : undeclared identifier`
  - `line 15: 'textureLod' : function not supported in this version`
- **Gutter markers in the CodeMirror editor** (via `@codemirror/lint`'s `lintGutter()`). A small marker sits in the gutter on each affected line; hover for the message, or look at the line directly — the editor draws a subtle squiggle on the offending text. Also flagged with a red dot on the **Live preview** tab label so you can spot trouble while you're on the Code tab.

The line number is mapped from emitted GLSL back to your source — the translator's [emit pass](/help/shaders/translator-subset) records the offset between the prelude and the start of your `fragment()` body and the runtime subtracts that on error parse.

When the translator itself rejects your source (banned feature, unsupported shader_type, etc.) — see [the unsupported pane entry](/help/shaders/unsupported-pane) — both surfaces fire too, with the parser's reason in the banner + a marker on the offending line.

## Re-translation cadence

150ms debounce on the editor's onChange. A burst of keystrokes coalesces into one parse + emit + WebGL recompile. The translator itself is microseconds (regex-driven); WebGL compile is the slow part (~10–50ms typically) and we don't want to fire it 30 times during a single thought. Re-translation happens whether you're looking at the Code tab or the Live preview tab — switching tabs is purely a visibility change, no remount.

## What the preview can't show

The preview compositor reproduces canvas_item's `blend_mix` default. Custom `render_mode` blend modes (`blend_add`, `blend_sub`, `blend_mul`, `blend_premul_alpha`) are parsed but not applied — the runtime always uses alpha-mix.

For shaders that depend on **vertex stage data** — anything with `varying`, anything that uses `MODEL_MATRIX` etc. in a vertex function — the preview can't reproduce because Bleepforge runs on a fixed full-screen quad. See [the translator subset reference](/help/shaders/translator-subset) for the full feature list.
